---
title: "Workflow: National BRFSS data"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Workflow: National BRFSS data}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment  = "#>",
  eval     = FALSE
)
```

This vignette walks an analyst working with CDC's national BRFSS data
through the brfssTools workflow, from a fresh install to a tidy tibble
ready for analysis. It assumes you don't have state-collected files of
your own — just CDC's public LLCP files.

The code chunks are not executed when this vignette is built; they're
documentation of the workflow against your real data on your real
machine.

## 1. Install the package

```r
install.packages("brfssTools")

# Or from a fork / development source:
# pak::pak("nfparsons/brfssTools")
```

A fresh install ships with the CDC codebook, taxonomy, and a sample
crosswalk. Everything else lives in your user config directory (see
step 3) where it persists across package upgrades.

## 2. Download CDC's national BRFSS data

The package can fetch the public LLCP files directly from CDC's
website:

```r
library(brfssTools)

brfss_download(2019:2024)
#> Downloading BRFSS 2019 from CDC...
#>   -> C:/Users/.../AppData/Local/R/cache/R/brfssTools/LLCP2019.XPT
#> Downloading BRFSS 2020 from CDC...
#>   -> C:/Users/.../AppData/Local/R/cache/R/brfssTools/LLCP2020.XPT
#> ...
#> Scanning pool for dataset 'National'...
#> Indexed 6 file(s) for dataset 'National'.
```

Two things happen:

1. Each year's `LLCP{YYYY}XPT.ZIP` is downloaded into the package's
   cache directory and the XPT extracted.
2. The cache is automatically registered as the `"National"` pool, so
   `brfss_pull()` can immediately find the files.

You can re-run `brfss_download()` later with additional years; already-
present files won't re-download unless you pass `overwrite = TRUE`.

Only years 2011 and later are supported — CDC's 2011 weighting change
(raking + cell phone frame) makes earlier files non-comparable.

To see what's currently registered:

```r
brfss_pool_status()
#> # A tibble: 6 x 3
#>    dataset   year  file
#>    <chr>    <int>  <chr>
#>  1 National  2019  C:/.../cache/R/brfssTools/LLCP2019.XPT
#>  2 National  2020  C:/.../cache/R/brfssTools/LLCP2020.XPT
#>  ...
```

The cache lives at `brfss_cache_dir()` — you can clean it up by hand
if disk space is an issue, or use `brfss_clear_pool("National")` to
deregister without deleting files.

## 3. Initialize a config

The package keeps a *config directory* that stores per-user crosswalks
and transformations. This is separate from the cache (which holds raw
data files). The config persists across R sessions and package
upgrades.

For National-only workflows, you can use the package's shipped Oregon
crosswalk as a starting point — most CDC core variables map to the
same column names, so the crosswalk is largely usable as-is for
National data:

```r
brfss_init_state("OR")
#> Initialized config for sample state 'OR' from package data.
#> Config dir: C:/Users/.../AppData/Roaming/R/config/R/brfssTools
```

If you'd rather keep config in a project folder (so it can be
git-tracked alongside an analysis), pass an explicit path:

```r
brfss_init_state("OR", path = "C:/projects/aces-paper/brfss_config")
```

## 4. Tour the crosswalk editor (optional)

The crosswalk is a CSV file you can edit by hand, but the GUI editor
is faster for exploration. It launches a local Shiny app:

```r
brfss_crosswalk_editor()
```

The editor shows every concept × year as a heatmap. Clicking a row
opens the edit panel on the right, where you can:

* See the CDC labels for the variable
* Edit the `concept_id` (the unified name brfss_pull uses)
* Click **Rename concept everywhere** to bulk-rename across all years

For a National-only workflow you'll mostly *read* the crosswalk rather
than edit it — the CDC variable names are the system of record.

## 5. Set up a transformation (race example)

Some variables don't map cleanly to a single column. Race is the
canonical example, since it's typically computed from `_MRACE1` and
`_HISPANC` using project-specific category definitions.

brfssTools handles these via *transformations*: small declarative
YAML files. Set up the race transformation with the CDC-style template:

```r
brfss_setup_race_map()
#> Created categorical map 'race' at:
#>   C:/.../config/R/brfssTools/transformations/race.yaml
```

The YAML opens in your editor. The default template implements CDC's
`_RACE` calculated variable (7 NH single-race + Hispanic + don't-know).
Edit it to match your project's category definitions if you need
something different.

When you save, the transformation is immediately available to
`brfss_pull()` as a column called `race`.

## 6. Pull data

Now you're ready for the main event:

```r
df <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  years    = 2022:2023,
  source   = "cdc"
)
#> # A tibble: ~880,000 x 8
#>     year SEQNO   `_STATE` ACEADNED GENHLTH AGE   SEX   race
#>    <int> <chr>     <chr>    <chr>    <chr> <chr> <chr> <chr>
#>  ...
```

A few things to notice:

* `source = "cdc"` is required for National data. The default auto-
  detects: if a state pool is registered it picks state, otherwise CDC.
  So if you ONLY have the National pool registered, you can omit this.
* `AGE` and `SEX` showed up automatically because
  `core_demographics = TRUE` is the default.
* `race` is computed via the YAML transformation you set up in step 5.
* `_STATE` is a FIPS code; useful for downstream state-level grouping.

For National data you'll usually want to filter to specific states:

```r
df_pnw <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  states   = c("OR", "WA", "ID"),
  years    = 2022:2023,
  source   = "cdc"
)
```

The `states` argument accepts USPS codes (like `"OR"`, `"WA"`) or FIPS
integers (`c(41L, 53L)`).

Other useful patterns:

```r
# Pull every concept in a domain:
df_bh <- brfss_pull(
  domains = "Behavioral Health",
  years   = 2022:2023,
  source  = "cdc"
)

# Combine concepts and a domain:
df_combined <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("AGE", "ACEADNED"),
  domains  = "Demographics",
  years    = 2023,
  source   = "cdc"
)

# Long-format output (one row per respondent x concept):
df_long <- brfss_pull(
  concepts = c("ACEADNED", "GENHLTH"),
  years    = 2022:2023,
  source   = "cdc",
  output   = "long"
)

# Skip the auto-included demographics:
df_minimal <- brfss_pull(
  concepts          = "ACEADNED",
  years             = 2023,
  source            = "cdc",
  core_demographics = FALSE
)
```

## 7. From here, your usual tidyverse workflow takes over

`brfss_pull()` returns a tibble. Survey design columns are named
`.weight`, `.strata`, and `.psu` (these come from CDC's `_LLCPWT`,
`_STSTR`, and `_PSU`). For National BRFSS data, all three are needed
to compute correct standard errors.

From here, it's any tidyverse-compatible package: `dplyr` and `tidyr`
for reshaping, `srvyr` or `survey` for weighted estimates, `ggplot2`
for visualization, `gtsummary` for tables.

## Troubleshooting

**`No pool registered for dataset 'National'`** — you skipped step 2,
or your R session restarted. Re-run `brfss_download()`.

**`brfssTools config dir not found`** — you skipped step 3. Run
`brfss_init_state("OR")`.

**Download fails with HTTP error** — CDC's URL format is fixed at
`https://www.cdc.gov/brfss/annual_data/{YYYY}/files/LLCP{YYYY}XPT.ZIP`.
Check whether you can access that URL in a browser. Corporate
proxies sometimes block large XPT downloads; try using a personal
network or pre-download the files manually and use
`brfss_set_pool("National", path)` instead.

**Editor won't launch** — make sure Shiny and friends are installed:

```r
install.packages(c("shiny", "DT", "bslib", "htmltools"))
```

**`Reading categorical maps requires the 'yaml' package`** — install it:

```r
install.packages("yaml")
```
